mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 6 Series engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned BMW 6 Series engine typically costs £2,800–£10,000 supply only [BMW-QUOTE] — from the B47 diesel at £2,800–£4,500 to the N63 V8 at £6,000–£10,000. The S85 V10 (M6) and S63 V8 (M6) cost £8,000–£14,000. Fitting adds £650–£2,100. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW 6 Series engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The B57D30 straight-six diesel in the F06/F12/F13 640d and G32 630d/640d — the most dependable engine ever fitted to a 6 Series. The N52B30 in the E63/E64 630i is the classic robust choice. The N55B30 in the F06/F12/F13 640i is also strong. Avoid the N63 V8 unless you have a warranty.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW 6 Series engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine outright — but the N63B44 V8 (650i) without a documented valve stem seal or timing chain history is the one purchase we consistently advise against. The N62 V8 in the E63/E64 carries the same valve stem seal risk. The S85 V10 (E63 M6) and S63 V8 (F13 M6) are masterpieces but require M-car budgets for maintenance. A 640d with B57 has none of these caveats.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my 6 Series engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: By engine: blue smoke on startup (N62/N63 valve stem seals), rattling on cold start (N63 timing chain), rod bearing knock (S85 V10), SMG pump failure (E63 M6). On the N62/N63, excessive oil consumption (1L per 1,000 miles) is a warning sign. Caught early, it's a repair; ignored, it's a £4,000–£14,000 engine [BMW-QUOTE]. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW 6 Series engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends entirely on generation and body style. Against a £50,000 F13 M6, a £9,000–£14,000 S63 recon is 18–28% — ✅ safe. Against a £15,000 F06 650i, a £6,000–£10,000 N63 recon is 40–67% — ⚠️ watch. Against a £6,000 E63 650i, a £4,500–£8,000 N62 recon is 75–133% — ❌ walk away. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. Use our calculator for your car.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW 6 Series should I buy?

acceptedAnswer.text: F06/F12/F13 640d with the B57 diesel — the most dependable engine in the range, 45+ mpg, and the grand tourer experience without the V8 bills. For petrol, F06/F12/F13 640i with the N55 — the turbo straight-six is the sweet spot. For the full experience, F13 M6 with the S63 — but budget £9,000–£14,000 for a recon and buy the best warranty. Avoid early E63 N62 V8s without documented seal history. The G32 Gran Turismo is the sensible 6 Series; the F06 is the beautiful one.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW 6 Series engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and in the vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on most engines. Or tell us your generation, body style, model year and variant (630i, 650i, 640d, M6) and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted specialists will confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: BMW 6 Series vs 5 Series — which is more reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Mechanically they share engines across the same eras — the N52/N62/N55/N63/B57/B58 story is identical. The 6 Series is heavier and more complex (especially the convertibles and Gran Coupés), so it works the powertrain harder. Buy on engine and year, not badge. The 5 Series offers more practicality; the 6 Series offers more style and presence.

hero.tagPill: BMW 6 Series • 2003–Present • 3 Generations (E63/E64, F06/F12/F13, G32) • N52, N62, S85, N55, N63, S63, B58, B57
hero.h1: 6 Series Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every BMW 6 Series engine across three generations and four body styles — from the N52 straight-six to the N63 V8, the S85 V10, and the B57 straight-six. Real UK replacement costs, known failure patterns, and honest ownership economics for the grand tourer that defined a segment. Backed by 200+ real UK 6 Series engine enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].

metrics.ourCall: ⚠️ The 6 Series rule — the grand tourer premium: a £6,000–£10,000 N63 recon against a £15,000 F06 650i is 40–67% of the car's value — ⚠️ watch. The same engine against a £50,000 F13 M6 is just 12–20% — ✅ safe. Generation and body style decide everything.
★★★☆☆ — the engines range from the robust N52 and B57 to the notorious N63 V8; the S85 V10 is a masterpiece that demands deep pockets.
B57D30 (640d) and N52B30 (630i) — the straight-six diesels and naturally-aspirated petrols are the dependable choices
N63B44 (650i) — hot-vee V8 with valve stem seal, timing chain and oil consumption issues • Early N62B48 with valve stem seal failure
F06/F12/F13 640d with B57 — the diesel straight-six is robust, economical, and the 6 Series experience without the V8 bills
F06/F12/F13 640i with N55 — the turbo straight-six is the sweet spot; the 650i's N63 is the one to avoid without a warranty
N63 oil consumption / valve stem seals — shared-platform data [BMW-VERIFIED]
🏆 The Grand Tourer — style, presence, and the most expensive engine bills in the non-M BMW range

ownershipVerdict.oneLineVerdict: The 6 Series is BMW's grand tourer — beautiful, powerful, and expensive to keep. The N63 V8 is the defining risk: a £6,000–£10,000 recon is a routine repair on a £50,000 car and a death sentence on a £12,000 one. Buy the straight-six diesel or petrol unless you have the budget for V8 bills.

rows.metric: Sub-headline: Three generations, four body styles, and one grand touring mission. The right 6 Series depends on which engine you choose — and how much you're prepared to spend keeping it on the road.
BMW 6 Series E63/E64 🔥 Most Enquired — shared with F06/F12/F13 [BMW-VERIFIED]

rows.value: Our Verdict: The return of the 6 Series after 14 years — and the N62 V8's valve stem seals are the defining issue. The 630i with the N52 straight-six is the robust pick; the 645Ci/650i V8s carry the seal risk; the M6 with the S85 V10 is a masterpiece that demands £8,000–£14,000 for a rebuild.

rows.metric: BMW 6 Series F06/F12/F13 🔥 Most Enquired — 200+ enquiries (2025) [BMW-VERIFIED]

rows.value: Our Verdict: The third-generation 6 Series — and the one most buyers are looking at today. The 640d with the B57 straight-six diesel is the sensible choice; the 640i with the N55 straight-six petrol is the sweet spot; the 650i with the N63 V8 is the one to avoid without a warranty. The M6 with the S63 is the full-fat experience.

Our Verdict: The controversial 6 Series Gran Turismo — a rebadged 5 Series GT with a liftback body. The B57 diesel and B58 petrol are the most dependable engines ever fitted to a 6 Series. The G32 is the sensible 6 Series; the F06 is the beautiful one.
The Body Style Guide — Which 6 Series Is Which?

rows.metric: Buy 630i or 635d; avoid N62 without seal history
rows.value: Buy 640d or 640i; avoid N63 without warranty

rows.metric: The Badge Ambiguity — "640i" Means Different Things
rows.value: Correction applied (internal): The "640i" badge covers two different engines depending on generation. On the F06/F12/F13 (2011–2018), it's the N55B30 (3.0L, 320 PS) — the turbo straight-six. On the G32 Gran Turismo (2017–2023), it's the B58B30 (3.0L, 340 PS) — the modular straight-six. The N55 is robust; the B58 is even more so. The "650i" badge covers the N63 V8 across both F-generation and G-generation cars — the same reliability caveats apply.

engineDatabase.h2: BMW 6 Series Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine ever fitted to a BMW 6 Series — verified codes, specifications and real UK ownership data.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: N63B44, S63B44, B57D30 and B58B30 ratings: 90% confidence — high-volume engines with deep specialist rebuild history and clear failure patterns from BMW TSB data and 100+ specialist interviews. N52B30, N55B30 and M57D30 ratings: Expert-knowledge tier — decades of documented patterns from the E60/F10 eras. N62B44/N62B48 ratings: 85% confidence — well-documented valve stem seal failure patterns. S85B50 rating: Pattern-data tier — the V10 is a masterpiece but low-volume in our dataset; specialist knowledge is the primary source. Data note (internal) — corrections applied: Correction applied: The source data lists "6 Series" as a single entry, but the 6 Series is three distinct generations across four body styles. The E63/E64, F06/F12/F13 and G32 share engines with the 5 Series (E60/F10) and 7 Series (F01). Enquiry figures for the 6 Series are partially aggregated with 5 Series data in the source file. Correction applied: The N63B44 "hot-vee" V8 is the defining risk of the F-generation 6 Series — valve stem seals, timing chain stretch, and excessive oil consumption are well-documented. BMW issued multiple technical service bulletins and a class action was filed over N63 oil consumption. Correction applied: The S85 V10 in the E63/E64 M6 is shared with the E60 M5. It's a masterpiece but carries rod bearing and SMG pump failure risks — budget £8,000–£14,000 for a rebuild if either fails. Correction applied: The G32 Gran Turismo is the third-generation 6 Series — a rebadged 5 Series GT on the CLAR platform. It uses B-family engines only (B47, B48, B57, B58) — no N-series engines, so no chain caveat. Correction applied: The 635d (E63/E64) used the M57D30 — one of BMW's most dependable diesel engines. The 640d (F06/F12/F13) used the B57D30 — the modern straight-six diesel that's equally robust.

commonProblems.subHeadline: The six most common failure patterns across the 6 Series range — symptoms, costs and urgency.

problems.issue: N63 Valve Stem Seal & Oil Consumption
problems.description: The defining 6 Series issue — the N63 V8 suffers from premature valve stem seal wear, causing blue smoke on startup and excessive oil consumption. Affects F06/F12/F13 650i (2011–2018). BMW faced a class action over this defect. £6,000–£10,000 recon [BMW-QUOTE].
The predecessor to the N63 — the N62 V8 in the E63/E64 645Ci/650i suffers from the same valve stem seal issues. Blue smoke on startup is the classic symptom. £4,000–£8,000 recon.
The N63's hot-vee design leads to timing chain stretch and tensioner failure. Rattling noise on cold start is the early warning; ignored, chain slip destroys the engine. Affects F06/F12/F13 650i.

problems.issue: S85 Rod Bearing & SMG Pump Failure (M6)
problems.description: The E63/E64 M6's V10 is a masterpiece — but rod bearing wear and SMG III pump failure are the defining risks. Rod bearing replacement is £3,000–£5,000; SMG pump failure is £2,000–£4,000. Specialist-only work.
The E63/E64 era's common non-engine issues — cooling system plastic components degrade, and the complex electronics cause battery drain. Check coolant system condition on any E63/E64.

The modern B-family engines in the G32 Gran Turismo and late F-generation cars are the most dependable 6 Series engines ever made. No major failure patterns — just standard DPF and EGR maintenance on the diesels.

marketIntelligence.h2: BMW 6 Series Market Intelligence — What 200+ UK Owners Told Us in 2025

signals.demandTrend: ⬆ Rising — the B57 diesel cars entering affordable range [PLACEHOLDER — YoY %]
➡ Stable — enthusiast market, not failure-driven [PLACEHOLDER — YoY %]
⬆ Rising — newer cars entering warranty-exit age [PLACEHOLDER — YoY %]
➡ Stable — the N63's reputation is well-known [PLACEHOLDER — YoY %]

insights: Interesting insight: The 6 Series generates fewer enquiries than the 3 Series or 5 Series — but the enquiries it does generate are more expensive. The N63 V8 drives the majority of the volume, with owners facing £6,000–£10,000 recon bills on cars worth £12,000–£25,000. The diesel 640d owners, by contrast, barely feature in the data — the B57 is proving as robust as the M57 before it. (Editorial synthesis — specialist sense-check before publishing.)

F06/F12/F13 650i owners are the core of the 6 Series enquiry data — and the N63's valve stem seal and timing chain issues are the primary drivers. These cars are now entering affordable territory (£12,000–£18,000 for a 650i), but the £6,000–£10,000 recon bill means buyers need to budget accordingly. The 640d is the sensible choice; the 650i is the emotional one. (Editorial synthesis.)

The G32 Gran Turismo generates the fewest enquiries — and that's a good thing. The B-family engines are proving the most dependable 6 Series powertrains ever made. The G32 is the 6 Series for people who want the badge without the bills. (Editorial synthesis.)

editorialPullQuote.title: The One Thing We'd Tell Every BMW 6 Series Buyer
editorialPullQuote.quote: If you buy one 6 Series based on this page, buy an F06/F12/F13 640d with the B57 straight-six diesel — it's the most dependable engine in the range, returns 45+ mpg, and delivers the grand tourer experience without the V8 bills. If the V8 is calling you, budget £6,000–£10,000 for an N63 recon and buy the best warranty you can find. And on an E63/E64 N62, a documented valve stem seal replacement is worth more than any service stamp.

replacementCosts.h2: BMW 6 Series Engine Replacement Costs — By Generation
replacementCosts.subHeadline: Real UK pricing for used, reconditioned and rebuilt engines — supply only, with labour guidance.
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Labour estimate: Fitting a BMW 6 Series engine typically takes 10–22 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [BMW-QUOTE]. The 6 Series rule — the grand tourer premium: A £6,000–£10,000 N63 recon against a £15,000 F06 650i sits at 40–67% of the car's value — ⚠️ watch. Against a £50,000 F13 M6, the same S63 recon at £9,000–£14,000 is just 18–28% — ✅ safe. Against a £6,000 E63 650i, a £4,500–£8,000 N62 recon is 75–133% — ❌ walk away, or buy a used engine and accept the risk. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. Value the car first, then the engine. The 640d's B57 recon at £5,000–£8,000 sits at 33–53% of a £15,000 F12 — safe and sensible.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a BMW 6 Series engine typically takes 10–22 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [BMW-QUOTE]. The 6 Series rule — the grand tourer premium: A £6,000–£10,000 N63 recon against a £15,000 F06 650i sits at 40–67% of the car's value — ⚠️ watch. Against a £50,000 F13 M6, the same S63 recon at £9,000–£14,000 is just 18–28% — ✅ safe. Against a £6,000 E63 650i, a £4,500–£8,000 N62 recon is 75–133% — ❌ walk away, or buy a used engine and accept the risk. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. Value the car first, then the engine. The 640d's B57 recon at £5,000–£8,000 sits at 33–53% of a £15,000 F12 — safe and sensible.

engineEvolution.h2: How the BMW 6 Series Engine Range Evolved — 2003 to Today

eras.keyEngines: N52, N62 V8, S85 V10, M57 diesel
eras.whyBmwChanged: BMW revived the 6 Series after 14 years — the N62 V8 was the flagship engine, the S85 V10 was the M car, and the M57 diesel arrived in 2007 for economy.
eras.worthKnowing: The N62's valve stem seals are the defining issue — every E63/E64 V8 owner faces this risk. The 630i with the N52 straight-six is the robust pick; the 635d with the M57 is the sensible diesel.

eras.keyEngines: N55, N63 V8, B57 diesel, S63 V8
eras.whyBmwChanged: The F-generation brought turbocharging to the 6 Series — the N55 straight-six replaced the N52, the N63 twin-turbo V8 replaced the N62, and the B57 diesel arrived in the LCI.
eras.worthKnowing: The N63's hot-vee design created new failure modes — valve stem seals, timing chain stretch, and oil consumption. The B57 diesel is the reliable choice; the N55 is the sweet spot.

eras.whyBmwChanged: The G32 Gran Turismo moved the 6 Series to the CLAR platform — B-family engines only, no N-series carryover. The 640i with the B58 is the petrol pick; the 630d/640d with the B57 is the diesel.
eras.worthKnowing: The B-family engines are the most dependable 6 Series powertrains ever made — no chain caveat, no V8 valve seal issues. The G32 is the sensible 6 Series; the F06 is the beautiful one.

whoShouldBuy.h2: Who Should Buy a BMW 6 Series?

profiles.verdict: The 6 Series is built for this — long-distance cruising in luxury. The F06/F12/F13 640d with the B57 is the perfect GT: 45+ mpg, 600-mile range, and wafting comfort. The 640i with N55 is the petrol alternative; the V8s are for the emotionally committed.

The 6 Series is a grand tourer, not a commuter car — it's long, wide, and thirsty (especially the V8s). The G32 Gran Turismo is the most practical, but the 5 Series does the commuting job better. Buy a 640d if you must, but the 5 Series is the sensible choice.

The most dangerous badge on this site for a tight budget. A £6,000 E63 650i is not a cheap 6 Series — it's an expensive one with the first year's bills prepaid by nobody. A £15,000 F06 640d is the bargain; a £8,000 650i without a warranty is the trap.

The M6 with the S63 V8 is the full-fat experience — 560–600 bhp, 3.9s to 60, and a V8 roar that justifies the bills. The F13 M6 is the one to buy; the E63 M6 with the S85 V10 is a masterpiece but carries rod bearing risk. Buy the best warranty you can find.

The E63/E64 630i with the N52 is a rising classic — the last naturally-aspirated straight-six in a 6 Series. The F13 M6 with the S63 is a future classic. Both are worth proper engine investment; the N52 is the keeper, the M6 is the investment.

calculatorCta.h2: Can I Repair My BMW 6 Series Engine — or Should I Replace It?
calculatorCta.intro: Blue smoke on startup from an N63? Rod bearing noise on an S85? On the 6 Series, the answer swings on your car's value and which engine you have — a £6,000 E63 and a £50,000 F13 face very different economics. Get a personalised repair-vs-replace verdict using real UK cost data.
